name: Bessie Smith
abstract: Bessie Smith (April 15, 1892 – September 26, 1937) was an African-American blues singer widely renowned during the Jazz Age. Nicknamed the "Empress of the Blues" and formerly Queen of the Blues, she was the most popular female blues singer of the 1930s.

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Muranyi
about_quotes.evidence: He used to work for Frank Walker in the 20s, and Frank Walker was the recording director at Columbia Records and he recorded Bessie Smith. And he told me this marvelous story about that Bessie was trying to suck up to Frank Walker...
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Muranyi interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 11, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Pug Horton
about_quotes.evidence: I heard a recording of Bessie Smith... I didn’t understand... It was like a riveting sound. And I knew that I wanted to do something that would bring me closer to learning more about what was going on there.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pug Horton interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 22, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] He did a lot of stuff behind Bessie Smith, didn't he? [INTERVIEWEE] He did almost all her records. He played trombone on all her records.

about_quotes.speaker: James Lincoln Collier
about_quotes.evidence: here’s Bessie Smith. And my eyes popped open because when I went to high school I was the only kid in high school who knew anything about jazz.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Lincoln Collier interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Christopher "Happy" Goldston
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] I just have heard about that number. In fact, Clarence Williams played on a record of it, I think, with Bessie Smith, I believe.

about_quotes.speaker: Harry "Sweets" Edison
about_quotes.evidence: My mother had music all the time, all kinds of records, you know of Louis Armstrong, Bessie Smith, Mamie Smith, all the blues... she had it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry "Sweets" Edison interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995
